name: UK GDPR + Data Protection Act 2018
website_obligation: Privacy notice (Art.13/14 disclosures), lawful basis per processing, data-subject-rights mechanism (access/erasure/portability/objection), security of processing, controller/DPO identity, ICO registration where required.

detection.logic: Parse policy for controller identity, lawful basis, retention, rights contact, intl transfer mechanism

detection_rules.description: Purposes of processing AND legal basis
detection_rules.layman_explanation: You do not state WHY data is collected or under WHICH lawful basis (consent, contract, legitimate interests, legal obligation, vital interests, public task). Article 13(1)(c) requires both. This is one of the ICO's most-cited breaches.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts a purposes-and-lawful-basis table per processing activity (marketing, support, payments, analytics) and embeds it in the privacy notice.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: ICO v Easylife 2022: £130,000 for invalid legitimate interest

detection_rules.description: Legitimate interests (if relied on) clearly stated
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Where you rely on "legitimate interests" as the lawful basis, Article 13(1)(d) requires you to spell out what those interests are. Saying just "for our legitimate business interests" is not enough.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the Legitimate Interests Assessment summary and the specific interests pursued, linked from the privacy notice.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Easylife — failed legitimate interest balancing test

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (recipients?|third parties|service providers|processors|sub[- ]?processors?|we share|with whom we share)
detection_rules.description: Recipients or categories of recipients of personal data
detection_rules.layman_explanation: You do not say who receives the personal data (processors, third parties, sub-processors). Article 13(1)(e) requires categories of recipient. Visitors and the ICO use this to assess data-sharing risk.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the recipients/processors disclosure (cloud hosting, analytics, email, payment) and links to the sub-processor register.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Various

detection_rules.description: International transfers and safeguards (SCCs / adequacy)
detection_rules.layman_explanation: You do not disclose international transfers of personal data outside the UK/EEA. Article 13(1)(f) requires you to name the destination country and the safeguard (adequacy decision, SCCs, BCRs). Post-Schrems II, ICO scrutinises this hard.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the international-transfers table (destination, transfer mechanism, link to SCCs/Adequacy regulation).
detection_rules.enforcement_example: ICO v TikTok 2023: £12.7m, partly for unclear transfer rules

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (retention period|how long we (keep|retain)|data retention|retain.{0,30}years?|for as long as|kept for)
detection_rules.description: Retention period or criteria
detection_rules.layman_explanation: You do not state how long personal data is retained. Article 13(2)(a) requires either a specific period or the criteria used to determine it. Indefinite retention is itself a breach.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts a retention schedule per data category (e.g. marketing 24 months, accounting 7 years) and adds it to the privacy notice.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: ICO v Clearview 2022: £7.5m for indefinite retention

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (right to (access|rectification|erasure|restrict|object|portability)|your rights under|exercise your rights|data subject rights)
detection_rules.description: Right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Visitors cannot find their statutory rights: access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection. Article 13(2)(b) requires you to enumerate them and explain how to exercise each.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the data-subject-rights section (one paragraph per right) plus the rights request workflow (email + verification flow).
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Multiple cases

detection_rules.description: Right to withdraw consent (if consent is the basis)
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Where processing is based on consent, Article 13(2)(c) requires you to say how that consent can be withdrawn. Withdrawal must be as easy as giving consent.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a "How to withdraw your consent" block and wires a one-click withdrawal route into the preference centre.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Various

detection_rules.description: Right to complain to ICO
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Article 13(2)(d) requires you to tell visitors they can complain to the ICO. The link to ico.org.uk must be present and clickable.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the ICO complaints disclosure with a live link to ico.org.uk/make-a-complaint.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Various

detection_rules.description: Whether provision is statutory/contractual and consequences of not providing
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Where data collection is required by law or by a contract, Article 13(2)(e) requires you to say so and explain the consequences of not providing it.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the statutory-or-contractual-requirement disclosure per form/data-point.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Various

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (automated decision|profiling|logic involved|significance and the envisaged consequences)
detection_rules.description: Existence of automated decision-making including profiling
detection_rules.layman_explanation: If you use automated decision-making (including profiling for marketing, pricing, eligibility), Article 13(2)(f) requires meaningful information about the logic involved and the consequences.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the automated-decisioning disclosure and the human-review opt-out route.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: ICO guidance on AI

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (data controller|controller is|controller:|registered office.*for the purposes of)
detection_rules.description: Identity of the controller must be disclosed on first data collection page
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Your privacy notice does not name who the legal "data controller" is. Visitors cannot tell who is collecting their data. ICO treats this as a first-mover breach of Article 13(1)(a) and opens an inquiry within weeks.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the controller-identity disclosure block (registered name, company number, registered office, ICO registration) and inserts it as paragraph 1 of the privacy notice.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: ICO v doorstep canvasser 2023: £130,000 for failing to identify controller

detection_rules.description: Contact details of the controller (including DPO if applicable)
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Visitors cannot find a contact route for the Data Protection Officer (or controller equivalent). Article 13(1)(b) requires an email or postal address dedicated to DPO queries.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a DPO contact block with a dedicated privacy@ or dpo@ inbox and the DPO's name where one is appointed.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Same

detection_rules.description: UK right of access (Subject Access Request)
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK residents can make a Subject Access Request; the route is not explained.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a UK SAR route.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 15; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (right to object|object to (processing|direct marketing)|opt[- ]out of marketing)
detection_rules.description: UK right to object to direct marketing
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK residents can object to direct marketing; not disclosed.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the objection right and opt-out.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 21; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

detection_rules.description: UK records of processing (ROPA)
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK GDPR Art. 30 ROPA expected; none referenced.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia maintains the ROPA.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 30; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

detection_rules.description: UK right to erasure
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK residents can request erasure; the route is not explained.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a UK erasure route.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 17; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

detection_rules.description: UK right to data portability
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK GDPR portability is not disclosed.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a portability route.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 20; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

detection_rules.description: UK lawful basis identified
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK GDPR Art. 6 requires a stated lawful basis; not identified.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia documents the UK lawful basis per activity.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 6; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

detection_rules.description: UK processor / sub-processor transparency
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK GDPR Art. 28 processor transparency is not addressed.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia documents processors and sub-processors.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 28; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

name: Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003
website_obligation: Prior informed active consent before non-essential cookies; soft opt-in for e-mail marketing to existing customers of similar products; granular reject-all as prominent as accept-all; no cookie wall.
max_penalty: Historically £500k; raised toward UK GDPR levels by Data (Use and Access) Act 2025

detection.logic: Playwright clean-load; intercept all Set-Cookie + JS cookie writes pre-interaction; classify marketing/analytics/essential
Check banner for pre-ticked boxes, reject-all parity, cookie-wall

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (reject all|reject non[- ]essential|decline all|refuse all|reject cookies|necessary (cookies )?only|only essential|manage preferences)
detection_rules.description: Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All. This is a specific requirement of UK PECR, which the ICO enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and func" on your site so it satisfies UK PECR.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: DUAA 2025 raised the maximum PECR fine from 500000 to 17.5M or 4 percent of global turnover. The ICO is sweeping the top 1000 UK sites; in its first batch of 200, 134 firms were warned and given 30 days to fix non-compliant banners.

detection_rules.description: Marketing or analytics trackers present but no consent-management platform gating them before consent
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Marketing or analytics trackers present but no consent-management platform gating them before consent. This is a specific requirement of UK PECR, which the ICO enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Marketing or analytics trackers present but no consent-management platform gating them bef" on your site so it satisfies UK PECR.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Both the SHEIN and American Express PECR cases involved cookies being set despite users clicking Reject All. The ICO treats non-functional or absent consent gating on analytics and advertising trackers as a consent violation.

name: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025
website_obligation: Raises PECR penalty caps to UK GDPR levels; adjusts cookie consent exemptions for low-risk analytics; recognised legitimate interests. Phased June 2025–June 2026.
max_penalty: Up to £17.5m or 4% global turnover (new PECR ceiling)

detection.logic: Re-baseline cookie consent against DUAA low-risk-analytics exemptions

name: Companies Act 2006 s.82 — Trading Disclosures
website_obligation: Display registered company name, registered number, registered office address, place of registration on the website. LLP = LLP number. Sole trader = name + principal place of business.
max_penalty: Fine up to £1,000 per officer; criminal offence

detection.logic: Regex footer for company number [0-9]{7,8}, 'Registered in', registered address
Verify number vs Companies House API matches trading name

detection_rules.description: VAT-registered businesses must show VAT number on website per HMRC guidance.
detection_rules.layman_explanation: If you collect VAT, HMRC expects your VAT number on customer-facing documents and your website. Missing it is a low-fine offence but triggers HMRC visits.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the VAT number to the footer and to the invoicing template.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Companies House: late-filing penalties, director disqualification and prosecution.

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (company (registration )?(no|number)|registered in (england|scotland|wales|northern ireland)|registered office|company no\.?|registered number|incorporated in)
detection_rules.description: Incorporated entity not displaying company number, registered office and place of registration on its website
detection_rules.layman_explanation: A UK limited company must print its company number, where it is registered and its registered office on the website. Many firms only put the trading name, which is an offence.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Add the full trading-disclosure block (registered name, number, place of registration, registered office) to the footer or contact page.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: The Companies (Trading Disclosures) Regulations 2015 and Companies Act 2006 s82 require a company to show its registered name, number, place of registration and registered office address on its website. Failure is an offence carrying a 1000 pound fine plus 100 pounds a day.

detection_rules.description: VAT number displayed where VAT-registered
detection_rules.layman_explanation: If VAT-registered you must display your VAT number on the website. It is not shown.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the VAT number to the footer and invoices.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: HMRC requires VAT-registered businesses to show the VAT number on their website and invoices.

detection_rules.description: UK limited companies must display registered name, number, registered office on every website.
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Every UK limited company is legally required to display its registered name, company number and registered office on its website. Missing it triggers fines from Companies House and breaches Companies Act 2006 s.82.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a single compliant disclosure block to the footer site-wide. 1-day fix.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Companies House: late-filing penalties, director disqualification and prosecution.

name: Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002
website_obligation: Provider must display name, geographic address, email for rapid contact, VAT number if registered, trade register number if applicable, supervisory authority if regulated; clear order-process steps.

detection.logic: Crawl contact/about for physical address, email, VAT number
Verify contact page 200 with full details

name: Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation & Additional Charges) Regulations 2013
website_obligation: Pre-contract information before online purchase; 14-day cancellation right for most distance contracts; model cancellation form; total price incl. taxes before payment; no pre-ticked add-ons.
max_penalty: Up to £5,000; contract may be unenforceable

detection.logic: On checkout verify total price incl VAT before payment; check withdrawal info
Verify cancellation/returns policy linked from product pages

name: Consumer Rights Act 2015
website_obligation: Terms must be fair and transparent; no unfair contract terms; goods/services/digital content as described and satisfactory quality. Unfair terms unenforceable.
max_penalty: Unenforceable terms; CMA action; consumer claims

detection.logic: Analyse T&Cs for unfair/disproportionate clauses, hidden liabilities

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (14[- ]day|cooling[- ]off|right to cancel|returns? policy|refund policy|cancellation rights|distance selling)
detection_rules.trigger_pattern: (add to (cart|basket)|checkout|buy now|place order|online order|subscribe|purchase online|shop now)
detection_rules.description: Online sales with no visible 14-day cancellation, returns or refund rights for distance consumers
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Selling online to UK consumers means you must tell them about their 14-day right to cancel and your returns and refund policy before they buy.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Publish clear cancellation, returns and refund terms and surface them at checkout.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: The Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013 give distance-selling consumers a 14-day cancellation right that must be communicated; the Consumer Rights Act 2015 governs refunds. Trading Standards enforce and the DMCC raises consumer-law fines to 10 percent of turnover.

detection_rules.description: Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires clear rights notice on commercial websites.
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK consumer-facing sites must publish their CRA 2015 rights notice including 14-day cooling-off, refund + return policies, and faulty-goods rights. CMA actively cross-references CRA with DMCC.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the CRA-compliant consumer rights page, links it from the footer + checkout, and aligns the returns flow to statutory wording.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Trading Standards enforcement and civil claims under the Consumer Rights Act 2015.

detection_rules.description: Fair, transparent and legible consumer contract terms
detection_rules.layman_explanation: The Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires consumer terms to be fair and in plain language; unfair terms are unenforceable. Yours are missing or opaque.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts fair, plain-language consumer terms.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: Consumer Rights Act 2015: unfair or unintelligible terms are unenforceable and draw Trading Standards action.

name: Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008
section_ref: SI 2008/1277 (now read with DMCC Act 2024 for fake reviews/drip pricing)
website_obligation: No misleading actions/omissions; no false urgency; no omission of material information; no undisclosed paid endorsements.
max_penalty: Unlimited fine; up to 2 years imprisonment

detection.logic: Flag unsubstantiated superlatives, false urgency, hidden fees, fake/undisclosed endorsements

name: Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024
section_ref: DMCC Act 2024 Part 4; commenced SI 2025/272 (6 Apr 2025); subscription rules expected Spring 2026
website_obligation: Ban on fake/incentivised reviews; platforms must take reasonable steps to prevent fake reviews; total price (incl. mandatory fees) at invitation-to-purchase (anti-drip-pricing); subscription transparency + easy exit (Spring 2026, pending).
max_penalty: Up to 10% global turnover or £300,000 for individuals

detection.logic: Detect review widgets; check verification disclosure; check drip pricing at add-to-cart vs checkout

detection_rules.description: Must publish a policy declaring no incentivised, fake or undisclosed reviews.
detection_rules.layman_explanation: From 2025, hosting fake or incentivised reviews without disclosure is a CMA fineable offence. The fine reaches £300k or 10% turnover. Buyers and platforms are both in scope.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts a Reviews Policy page meeting CMA wording, runs a review-audit on the live site, and updates the review-collection flow to meet disclosure standards.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024 (in force 6 April 2025): direct fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (incentivi|in exchange for|gifted|sponsored|disclosure|we verify|verified (review|purchase)|independently collected|genuine review|honest review)
detection_rules.description: Reviews or testimonials shown without disclosing incentives or a genuine-review verification process
detection_rules.layman_explanation: If you display reviews or testimonials, you must not feature fake ones and must disclose any that were incentivised or gifted. The CMA can fine up to 10 percent of worldwide turnover.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Add review-verification and an incentive-disclosure process, and remove any testimonials you cannot substantiate.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: The DMCC Act fake-review ban came into force on 6 April 2025; the CMA began enforcement from July 2025 and can fine up to 10 percent of global annual turnover without going to court. Businesses have a positive duty to prevent and remove fake and undisclosed-incentivised reviews.

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (total price|all[- ]in price|no hidden fees|all fees included|price includes (VAT|tax))
detection_rules.description: Total price including mandatory fees must be displayed up-front, not added at checkout.
detection_rules.layman_explanation: Drip pricing — adding mandatory fees at later stages — is now a banned practice under DMCC Part 4 (in force April 2025). Includes booking, service, processing and admin fees.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia audits the pricing journey, flags every drip charge, and rewrites the pricing/booking page to show full price up-front per CMA wording.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024 (in force 6 April 2025): direct fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

detection_rules.description: Clear subscription terms + easy cancellation (DMCC)
detection_rules.layman_explanation: The DMCC Act requires clear subscription terms, renewal reminders and easy cancellation; yours are unclear.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds DMCC-compliant subscription terms and easy cancellation.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: DMCC Act 2024 tightens subscriptions (clear terms, reminders, easy exit); CMA fines up to 10% of turnover.

detection_rules.regex_pattern: (cancel(\.| your)? subscription|subscription reminder|renewal notice|easy cancellation|cancel any[- ]?time)
detection_rules.description: Subscription traders must send reminders before renewal and offer one-click cancellation.
detection_rules.layman_explanation: If you charge customers on a subscription you must send pre-renewal reminders and let them cancel as easily as they signed up. CMA can fine up to £300k or 10% global turnover. From April 2025 these are direct CMA fines, no court order needed.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia audits the subscription flow, drafts the renewal-reminder template (24-hour rule), and rewrites the cancel page to meet CMA Part 4 wording.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024 (in force 6 April 2025): direct fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

name: Equality Act 2010 — Website Accessibility
section_ref: Equality Act 2010 s.20,29 (reasonable adjustments); WCAG 2.1 AA accepted standard
website_obligation: Reasonable adjustments so disabled users are not at substantial disadvantage; WCAG 2.1 AA is the accepted benchmark.

detection.logic: axe-core full WCAG 2.1 AA audit; report by impact + criterion
Check colour contrast, alt text, form labels, heading order

enforcement_feed: EHRC (no central feed); BAILII for case law

detection_rules.description: Customer-facing websites must offer reasonable adjustments under the Equality Act 2010.
detection_rules.layman_explanation: UK Equality Act 2010 imposes a reasonable-adjustments duty on every customer-facing website. EHRC published a 2024 code expecting WCAG 2.1 AA conformance + a published accessibility statement.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia ships a WCAG 2.1 AA audit + statement + the top 10 priority fixes. EHRC-format statement deployed to /accessibility within 4 weeks.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: EHRC enforcement and civil claims: uncapped tribunal awards for discrimination.

detection_rules.description: Image alt text as a reasonable adjustment
detection_rules.layman_explanation: The Equality Act 2010 requires reasonable adjustments including accessible content (alt text). Your images lack it, which courts treat as discrimination.
detection_rules.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds alt text and brings the site to WCAG 2.1 AA.
detection_rules.enforcement_example: The Equality Act 2010 treats an inaccessible customer website as failing the reasonable-adjustment duty; claims are rising.

name: Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) Accessibility Regulations 2018
website_obligation: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance + published accessibility statement in the required format.
max_penalty: Monitoring by GDS; publication of non-compliance

name: FTC Act Section 5 — Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices
website_obligation: No deceptive representations; clear and conspicuous disclosures; testimonials must disclose material connections; substantiation for all objective claims.
max_penalty: Up to $51,744 per violation per day
